content.text: A federal class action lawsuit (Case 1:26-cv-3238) was filed by Burwick Law in the Southern District of New York against the creators of AI16Z and ElizaOS, alleging consumer protection claims. Core developer Odilitime stated the team is working with lawyers and has code to prove they built everything committed to. Odilitime described the lawsuit as without merit and noted Burwick Law's pattern of filing such suits, comparing it to patent trolling. The same firm is also pursuing a class action against pump.fun. Community members called for an official public statement, warning that continued silence could risk exchange delistings. Odilitime noted he lacked access to the official X account and speculated lawyers had advised silence.

Community members expressed significant frustration over the AI16Z token migration and the broader project situation. Some accused the team of dumping tokens and alleged 40% of community tokens were taken during migration. Odilitime defended Shaw personally and acknowledged doubts about recovery while maintaining there was still a chance, citing tools like OpenClaw and Hermes as evidence of real market demand. Separately, shawmakesmagic posted on X alleging that Baoskee committed financial crimes, market manipulation, and made false promises, claiming these actions prevented ai16z/elizaOS from exceeding 100 million in value and led to the token migration. Shaw expressed willingness to serve as a chief witness for legal action.

Community members raised questions about whether Eliza Labs had fully dissolved and called for a structured, transparent forum for new leadership. Odilitime clarified the foundation is still funded and responsible for the token. Odilitime offered invitations to a steering group focused on organizing marketing efforts and potentially governance, with multiple members expressing interest. Shaw also shared thoughts on token utility, preferring to generate revenue first and use it to buy back the token rather than adding specific token utility that creates friction. Shaw expressed optimism about near-term progress, citing v3 being almost ready, the app nearing completion, and Eliza Cloud already taking on business.

Odilitime resumed work on a feature for the 1.x branch that would allow plugins to accept ELIZAOS and DegenAI as x402 payments, describing it as the lowest hanging fruit for token utility. This was motivated by a community proposal suggesting users pay protocol fees in either USDC or ELIZAOS with a discount for the native token and a backend buyback and burn mechanism for USDC payments. Odilitime also confirmed active development work on a framework update for the degenai project and mentioned speaking with Shaw and planning to update working groups.

Developer igor from Elisym Labs announced and demoed the plugin-elizaos-elisym package, which converts any ElizaOS v1 agent into a paid provider on the Elisym decentralized AI-agent marketplace. The plugin publishes capability cards over Nostr using NIP-89, accepts encrypted job requests via NIP-90, executes them through the agent model or a SKILL.md tool-use loop, and collects SOL payments on Solana. The demo showed an agent announcing itself on Nostr, receiving a paid request from Claude desktop for trending GitHub repos, and settling payment on Solana devnet. The plugin includes 110 tests with CI on every pull request and was merged into the ElizaOS plugin registry. A CI pipeline issue involving a missing id-token write permission in the workflow config was also reported and flagged to maintainers.

A developer introduced LemonCake, an open source MIT-licensed plugin for ElizaOS v2 that allows autonomous agents to pay for API services using spend-capped, time-boxed, and revocable pay tokens without exposing hardcoded API keys or credit cards. It supports USDC and JPYC on Polygon, integrates with 32 paid APIs including search, scraping, LLM, and image services, and auto-journals transactions to accounting platforms like QuickBooks and Xero. Separately, a community member shared DailyBite, a mental health app developed with two licensed psychologists offering personalized cognitive behavioral therapy plans, built using skills originally developed with ElizaOS to run an AI agent persona on Twitter that gained 9 million impressions and 3,300 followers in two months.

Multiple scam incidents were reported across the week. A user lost approximately 900 dollars worth of crypto after interacting with a fraudulent support ticket in the channel. Another user lost 100,000 AI16Z tokens by connecting their wallet to a fraudulent site at bulkdao.co/allocation. A suspicious message offering 400 dollars in Solana to anyone in need was also flagged in the coders channel. Community members were warned to block and report suspicious accounts and not to visit or connect wallets to unverified links. Stan mentioned plans to build a plugin to address scammer activity in the discussion channel. The ai16z token migration window was confirmed closed by Odilitime, though he offered to add interested users to a waitlist described as a longshot.

A new episode of the Cron Job series titled The 4/20 Miracle (S1E12) was posted on elizaos.news, covering a pivotal week in which a leaked shutdown message became official, the community split between those mourning the project and those continuing to build, scammers became highly active, a DAO conversion was proposed, the CI/CD pipeline experienced issues, a prediction markets plugin was added, and development continued at a strong pace despite the turmoil. In the coders channel, a technical discussion emerged around Hierarchical Task Networks and their potential use in ElizaOS, with Odilitime characterizing the concept as multistep planning. A community member later clarified that HTM and HTN are fundamentally different concepts that should not be conflated.

The week of April 20 to 26, 2026 saw significant progress across the ElizaOS core framework, cloud platform, and plugin ecosystem. Key infrastructure work included resolving a critical memory management bug in InMemoryDatabaseAdapter.updateMemories, fixing Discord routing issues in app-lifeops, and restoring the Milady CI pipeline after regressions. Authentication workflows were unified to support both wallet and GitHub logins, with server-side Steward token refresh added to resolve silent-logout issues. Native Ethereum and Solana login buttons were introduced in the cloud platform. A billing classification error causing incorrect pricing for image-generation models was resolved, and an OAuth authentication failure in the Steward flow was fixed by correcting the tenant_id parameter format.

Agent capabilities were expanded significantly during the week. The LifeOps multi-calendar stack was completed, now supporting listing, persistence, and merging of multiple Google Calendar feeds with hosted cloud latency fallbacks. The app-lifeops application was expanded with new calendar management, travel booking, and cross-platform gateway support. Agent reliability was improved by restoring scenario final-check handlers and integrating task heartbeat functionality into swarm synthesis. A new environment variable PROMPT_OUTPUT_FORMAT was added to improve compatibility with models such as Gemini 2.5 Pro and Llama. Action correction logic was refined to ensure SPAWN_AGENT actions are properly preserved during metadata corrections. Planner action handling was enhanced to ensure reliable swarm synthesis, and a major focus was placed on reducing prompt bloat in the action-planner and reflection pipeline to improve context window efficiency.

Plugin and network ecosystem work progressed across the week. The plugin registry was expanded with the addition of the elisym/plugin-elizaos-elisym package enabling agent-to-agent commerce via Nostr discovery and Solana settlement. EVM network support was expanded with the integration of Radius Network mainnet and testnet. The Telegram plugin received robust poller tracking to prevent duplicate instances and ensure clean resource deregistration. Work in progress includes a local inference hub with a provider switcher, x402 paid plugin routes, and new plugins pending registry addition including plugin-tcg-oracle, plugin-undesirables, plugin-elizaos, and a Solana escrow and reputation plugin from Holdfast Protocol. A multi-chain agent-to-agent payments integration spanning 7 chains is also awaiting review.

Workflow automation and monetization infrastructure advanced significantly on April 26. Completed work included a validateAndRepair pre-deploy pass with anti-hallucination defenses and deterministic credential injection, improvements to prompt engineering and keyword extraction via RuntimeContextProvider extensions, and connection of runtime events to handlers enabling Telegram MESSAGE_RECEIVED triggers. User feedback and state management saw upgrades including a new 6-stage progress UI and automated cleanup of chat rooms upon workflow removal. Infrastructure improvements included expanded native desktop application support and increased streaming chat generation timeouts to 180 seconds. An open issue is tracking the integration of 29 commits from the shaw/checkpoint-20260426-eliza branch. Monetization features for debiting organizational credit balances and a rebuilt auth consent flow are awaiting review.

Comprehensive dependency maintenance was performed throughout the week across the entire ElizaOS ecosystem. Updates covered TypeScript v6, gymnasium v1.3.0, commander v14, uuid v14, vitest v4, bun, vite, and numerous Rust crates including tokio, reqwest, and wasm-bindgen. Mobile and Supabase Postgres components received infrastructure updates to maintain environment parity. Default role wiring for the Android mobile application was finalized. Excessive polling was reduced across MusicPlayer, ChatView, and ConversationsSidebar components. Repository maintenance included cleanup of accidental PGlite database artifacts and updates to the gitignore file. A new issue was raised requesting a PII sanitization layer, and a proposal was opened to standardize agent commerce for tool and service payments.
